text: Chapter Two teaches chromatic enclosures—short phrases mixing scale notes and chromatic passing notes to emphasize target notes—grounded in the foundational rule that chromatic notes belong on off-beats in hardbop and bebop jazz. The master’s characteristic approach emphasizes legato technique with hammer-ons, pull-offs, and slides to accent passing notes softly while resolving to scale notes, keeping both on the same string where possible. Two-note and four-note enclosure constructions anchor the technique: two-note enclosures place a chromatic note and diatonic note around a triad tone in either order, while four-note enclosures align resolution with strong beats (1 and 3) and function either as suspended sounds or as tension-and-pull devices toward chord tones. Key harmonic moves include playing arpeggios from the 5th degree (with added 3rd) over II chords and arpeggios from the 3rd degree as a bebop device, with diatonic notes substituting when chromatic gaps don’t exist. Advanced placement—such as resolving enclosures on weak beats like beat 4 (following a beat 2 placement)—creates modern, unresolved melodic qualities reminiscent of Metheny, but the chapter closes with a cautionary principle: enclosures must remain anchored to chord and scale tones to avoid overuse and maintain their gravitational function.
